title: Macros should be avoided as replacements for typedef and using
description: Macros should not be used as replacements for "typedef" and "using"Analyze your codeconsistency-conventionalmaintainabilityCode Smellbad-practicecertpitfallWhy is this an issue?More InfoC provides a way of defining or aliasing a type throughtypedef. On top of it, C++ addsusingthat can do the same and more.Using a macro to define a type is inferior to the previous ways for two reasons:macros cannot be enclosed into scopes. Or at least, doing so is cumbersome and error-prone, as in that case, the macro needs to be defined and undefined manually.macros are handled by the preprocessor and are not understood by the compiler.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: #define INT_PTR int* // Noncompliant: using macro for type definition
#define CALLBACK_TYPE void(*)(int) // Noncompliant: macro instead of typedef/using
#define MyVector std::vector<int> // Noncompliant: should use type alias
compliant: typedef int* IntPtr; // Compliant: using typedef for type alias
using CallbackType = void(*)(int); // Compliant: using declaration for type alias
using MyVector = std::vector<int>; // Compliant: modern C++ type alias

checks.message: Avoid using macros as replacements for typedef or using declarations. Use typedef or using instead.
